title: C# SOLID — Paper 7
description: Covers ISP concepts, ISP refactoring, DIP concepts, DIP implementation, SRP concepts, Code Smells & SOLID Violations, Refactoring Techniques, Unit Testing & SOLID, SOLID Real C# Scenarios, and Dependency Injection Patterns.

questions.text: ST01-4: A class `InvoiceManager` handles invoice creation, PDF generation, and database persistence. According to SRP, what is the 'reason to change' analysis?

options.text: Only one — the invoice domain.
Three — changes to invoice rules, to PDF library, or to persistence mechanism each require editing InvoiceManager.
Two — business logic and technical concerns.
Zero — the class is small enough to be SRP compliant.

questions.text: ST11-4: Which form of dependency injection is best for dependencies that are only needed by a single method, not the whole class?

options.text: Constructor injection
Method injection — passing the dependency as a method parameter.
Property injection
Service locator

questions.text: ST19-9: In a SOLID-compliant codebase, which of the following most accurately describes why interfaces improve unit testability?

options.text: Interfaces cause the compiler to enforce test coverage.
Interfaces allow mocking frameworks to substitute test doubles for real implementations, isolating the unit under test.
Interfaces reduce the number of test cases needed.
Interfaces automatically generate unit tests.

questions.text: ST08-9: The CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern is a high-level application of which SOLID principle?

options.text: LSP — substituting queries with commands.
ISP — separating read (query) and write (command) interfaces so consumers depend only on what they use.
OCP — commands cannot be modified.
DIP — commands depend on abstractions.

questions.text: ST09-8: Which of the following is a correct description of the 'Dependency Inversion' aspect of DIP, as distinct from Dependency Injection?

options.text: Dependency Inversion is a tool (DI container); Dependency Injection is the principle.
Dependency Inversion is the principle of depending on abstractions; Dependency Injection is a technique for fulfilling that principle at runtime.
They are the same concept.
Dependency Inversion reverses the call order of methods.

questions.text: ST20-7: The following interface is defined: `interface IWorker { void Work(); void Eat(); void Sleep(); }` A `RobotWorker` class throws exceptions on `Eat()` and `Sleep()`. Which principle is violated?

options.text: OCP
ISP — RobotWorker is forced to implement methods irrelevant to its role.
DIP
LSP

questions.text: ST17-13: 'Inappropriate Intimacy' is a code smell where:

options.text: A class is too tightly coupled to a framework.
Two classes depend on each other's private internals, creating high coupling and fragile design.
A class has too many public methods.
A method has too many side effects.

questions.text: ST10-5: In the Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection framework, the `IServiceProvider` interface is used to:

options.text: Define which services should be registered.
Resolve services from the container at runtime, either directly or via constructor injection.
Register services with their lifetimes.
Validate that all registered services can be constructed.

questions.text: ST18-11: 'Collapse Hierarchy' refactoring is appropriate when:

options.text: A class hierarchy has too many levels and some levels add no distinct behavior.
Two sibling classes should be merged into one larger class.
An abstract class should become a concrete class.
An interface should be replaced with an abstract class.

questions.text: ST07-13: A developer must refactor an `IAnimal` interface that has grown too large. The correct ISP approach is to:

options.text: Mark optional methods with [Optional] attribute.
Identify client-specific roles and create a separate interface per role, allowing classes to implement only what is relevant.
Make all unneeded methods return default values.
Create a single abstract base class instead.

questions.text: ST01-5: Which of the following code metrics most directly indicates a potential SRP violation?

options.text: High cyclomatic complexity in one class.
A class with many dependencies and a large number of methods spanning multiple business domains.
Low code coverage.
A class that uses inheritance.

questions.text: ST11-5: Which of the following represents a problem with 'constructor over-injection' (too many constructor parameters)?

options.text: The DI container cannot resolve classes with more than two parameters.
Too many constructor parameters is a code smell indicating the class has too many responsibilities (SRP violation).
It prevents property injection from being used.
It causes circular dependency exceptions at compile time.

questions.text: ST20-8: A class `FileProcessor` reads a file, parses its content, validates data, and writes results to a database in a single `Process()` method. Which principles are violated?

options.text: OCP and ISP
SRP and DIP — multiple responsibilities and direct dependency on file system and database.
LSP and OCP
ISP and LSP

questions.text: ST09-9: An application layer class `OrderService` depends on `IEmailSender` (interface). The `SmtpEmailSender` class implements `IEmailSender`. This demonstrates DIP because:

options.text: OrderService (high-level) depends on IEmailSender (abstraction), and SmtpEmailSender (low-level) also depends on IEmailSender (abstraction).
SmtpEmailSender depends on OrderService.
IEmailSender depends on SmtpEmailSender's concrete methods.
OrderService creates SmtpEmailSender internally.

questions.text: ST19-10: In TDD (Test-Driven Development), how do SOLID principles emerge naturally?

options.text: Tests enforce SOLID compliance at compile time.
Writing tests first forces you to design loosely-coupled, single-responsibility classes that are easy to construct and verify in isolation.
TDD generates SOLID-compliant code automatically.
TDD requires classes to implement many interfaces, naturally enforcing ISP.

questions.text: ST08-10: An `INotificationService` interface has methods: `SendEmail()`, `SendSms()`, `SendPush()`, `SendFax()`. A client only needs to send emails. ISP-compliant refactoring would:

options.text: Create an IEmailNotification interface with only SendEmail(), letting the client depend on it.
Add an EmailOnly flag to INotificationService.
Mark unused methods with [Obsolete].
Move SendEmail() to a static helper class.

questions.text: ST17-14: 'Comments' can be a code smell when they are used to:

options.text: Explain why a non-obvious decision was made.
Compensate for poorly named variables, methods, or classes — indicating the code itself is unclear.
Document public API methods.
Add copyright notices to source files.

questions.text: ST10-6: The `IServiceCollection.AddScoped<T>()` method in ASP.NET Core creates one instance per:

options.text: Application lifetime.
HTTP request (or DI scope).
Method call.
Thread.

questions.text: ST20-9: A `PaymentProcessor` class has a method `Process(string type)` containing `if (type == "PayPal") ... else if (type == "Stripe") ...`. Which refactoring applies OCP?

options.text: Add more else-if branches and add comments explaining each.
Introduce an IPaymentStrategy interface with PayPalStrategy and StripeStrategy implementations; inject the chosen strategy.
Move the if-else into a switch expression.
Create a PaymentProcessorV2 class for each new provider.

questions.text: ST11-6: Which of the following is a risk of property injection compared to constructor injection?

options.text: Property injection is not supported by DI containers.
Property injection allows the object to be in an invalid state if the property is not set before use, as the dependency is not guaranteed at construction time.
Property injection is slower than constructor injection.
Property injection cannot be used with interfaces.

questions.text: ST01-6: In SRP, 'cohesion' refers to:

options.text: The number of classes in a module.
How strongly related and focused the responsibilities of a class or module are.
The degree to which classes depend on each other.
The number of public methods a class exposes.

questions.text: ST18-12: 'Remove Middle Man' refactoring is used when:

options.text: A class has too many responsibilities and should be split.
A class does little but delegate every call to another object, adding no value; clients can call the target directly.
An interface is used only once and should be removed.
A base class delegates all work to subclasses.

questions.text: ST07-14: Which of the following best explains why fat interfaces increase coupling?

options.text: Fat interfaces use generics, which increases compile time.
Clients that implement or depend on a fat interface are coupled to methods they never use, meaning changes to unused methods still affect them.
Fat interfaces cannot be registered in a DI container.
Fat interfaces prevent polymorphism.

questions.text: ST19-11: What is the difference between unit tests and integration tests in a SOLID codebase?

options.text: Unit tests test multiple classes; integration tests test one class.
Unit tests isolate a single unit (class/method) using test doubles; integration tests verify that multiple real components work together correctly.
Integration tests are always slower and less reliable than unit tests.
SOLID-compliant code only needs integration tests.

questions.text: ST09-10: A `ReportService` depends on `IDataSource` (interface) instead of `SqlDataSource` (class). If a new `CsvDataSource` is introduced, the `ReportService`:

options.text: Must be modified to support CsvDataSource.
Requires no changes — CsvDataSource implements IDataSource and can be injected without altering ReportService.
Must be recompiled even though it requires no source changes.
Cannot use CsvDataSource because it was not anticipated at design time.

questions.text: ST20-10: A `Product` class exposes 20 public properties and handles pricing, inventory, display, and serialization. Which principle does this most violate?

options.text: ISP — the product interface is too large.
SRP — a single class handles too many distinct concerns.
OCP — the product class cannot be extended.
DIP — the product depends on too many concrete types.

questions.text: ST10-7: What does 'composition root' mean in the context of dependency injection?

options.text: The base class from which all composable services derive.
The single place in an application (e.g., Program.cs or Startup.cs) where the entire dependency graph is assembled and registered.
A root interface that all DI-injectable classes must implement.
The first object created when the application starts.

questions.text: ST17-15: 'Refused Bequest' is a code smell where:

options.text: A class refuses to inherit from a base class.
A subclass inherits from a parent but ignores or overrides most of the inherited methods, suggesting the inheritance relationship is wrong.
A class refuses to implement an interface method.
A class refuses to be injected into other classes.

questions.text: ST08-11: Which of the following demonstrates a correct ISP application in C# for a payment system?

options.text: A single IPayment interface with Charge(), Refund(), Dispute(), Subscribe(), and Unsubscribe() methods for all consumers.
Separate IChargeable, IRefundable, and ISubscribable interfaces so different consumers depend only on the payments they initiate.
An abstract PaymentBase class with virtual methods for all payment operations.
A static PaymentHelper class with all payment methods.

questions.text: ST11-7: Which ASP.NET Core middleware feature is a real-world example of the Chain of Responsibility pattern, related to DI?

options.text: DbContext lifecycle management.
The middleware pipeline where each middleware is injected with IApplicationBuilder and can call the next component.
The appsettings.json configuration system.
The ILogger factory.
